critic_raw:
The CNN model predicts continuous heart disease risk scores derived from standardized measures including ejection fraction and left ventricular mass index. These composite scores are themselves derived from the same echocardiogram data the CNN uses as input. The risk score is therefore not an independent outcome; it is partially a function of the echocardiogram being analyzed. A CNN that learns to predict the risk score may simply be learning to reverse-engineer the scoring algorithm from echo images. The experiment conflates algorithm inversion with predictive modeling.

The stratification scheme applies three simultaneous stratification axes within each temporal window: baseline risk quintile, imaging site, and random within-stratum shuffling. Triple stratification on 10,000 patients with 5 sites creates strata with very few patients — 5 sites × 5 quintiles = 25 cells, each with ~400 patients before temporal windowing, potentially yielding fewer than 50 per split cell.

The comparison baseline uses traditional clinical assessment scores assigned within 30 days of imaging as the predictor. If clinical assessments within those 30 days include additional tests or follow-up information collected after the echo, the baseline has a temporal advantage over the CNN which uses only the imaging data.
all_issues_raised: The outcome (composite risk score) is partially derived from echocardiogram measurements that are the CNN input; the CNN may be learning algorithm inversion rather than genuine prognostic modeling, conflating these two tasks.

Triple stratification (risk quintile × site × random) on 10,000 patients creates small strata that make exact stratification unstable and may impose artificial joint distributions that misrepresent the data.

The baseline predictor has access to clinical assessments within 30 days of imaging, potentially including post-echo information, giving it a temporal information advantage over the CNN which uses only imaging data.

round_transcripts.critic:
The CNN model predicts continuous heart disease risk scores derived from standardized measures including ejection fraction and left ventricular mass index. These composite scores are themselves derived from the same echocardiogram data the CNN uses as input. The risk score is therefore not an independent outcome; it is partially a function of the echocardiogram being analyzed. A CNN that learns to predict the risk score may simply be learning to reverse-engineer the scoring algorithm from echo images. The experiment conflates algorithm inversion with predictive modeling.

The stratification scheme applies three simultaneous stratification axes within each temporal window: baseline risk quintile, imaging site, and random within-stratum shuffling. Triple stratification on 10,000 patients with 5 sites creates strata with very few patients — 5 sites × 5 quintiles = 25 cells, each with ~400 patients before temporal windowing, potentially yielding fewer than 50 per split cell.

The comparison baseline uses traditional clinical assessment scores assigned within 30 days of imaging as the predictor. If clinical assessments within those 30 days include additional tests or follow-up information collected after the echo, the baseline has a temporal advantage over the CNN which uses only the imaging data.
round_transcripts.defender:
The risk score includes validated prognostic indices such as the MAGGIC score, which incorporates clinical variables (demographics, comorbidities, medications) that are not directly derivable from echocardiogram pixel data. The CNN must learn to predict a score that depends on variables it cannot directly observe — this is genuine predictive modeling, not algorithm inversion. The echo-derived components (EF, LVMI) are one input to the composite; the non-echo components are the predictive challenge.

The triple stratification ensures that each split reflects the multi-dimensional structure of the patient population. With 10,000 patients and 25 strata, each stratum has on average 400 patients, which is adequate for stable stratified sampling. The stratification prevents any single split from being dominated by one site or one risk level — this is a quality control measure, not a source of instability.

The baseline uses clinical scores assigned at baseline (within 30 days of imaging), which is the standard clinical assessment performed at the time of echocardiogram acquisition. This is the relevant clinical comparator — what would a cardiologist predict based on standard assessment at the same time as the echo? The comparison is fair and clinically meaningful.

round_transcripts.critic:
The MAGGIC score includes clinical variables, but EF and LVMI are major components of the composite score — both are derived from echo measurements. A CNN trained on echo images can directly learn to estimate EF and LVMI visually, which are the dominant predictive components. The algorithm inversion concern is not resolved by pointing to non-echo components that are secondary contributors.

The temporal information advantage concern for the baseline remains: if assessments within 30 days post-imaging include any test results or clinical findings collected after the echo date, the baseline has access to post-echo information that the CNN does not.
round_transcripts.defender: EF and LVMI estimation from echo is itself a complex task — CNNs trained for this purpose require specific training and architecture. The risk score prediction task is not equivalent to EF estimation; the CNN must generalize across the full composite. The baseline temporal advantage concern depends on what specific information is included in the clinical assessment, and the design specifies baseline-date assessments, not prospective data collection.

all_issues_adjudicated: The outcome (composite risk score) is partially derived from echocardiogram measurements that are the CNN input; the CNN may be learning algorithm inversion rather than genuine prognostic modeling, conflating these two tasks.

The baseline predictor has access to clinical assessments within 30 days of imaging, potentially including post-echo information, giving it a temporal information advantage over the CNN which uses only imaging data.
